paragraphs.text: I would like to add another highly important matter: Selling licences disguised as products/goods. This applies to most digital sales of video games and e-books from my own experience and relates closest to "Obfuscation Through Legalese" mentioned in the Mission Letter. Digital storefronts should make it crystal clear to the customer whether they're purchasing a licence (most often revocable) or actual digital ownership of a good (or a perpetual, non-revocable license). There have been many cases where customers have suddenly lost access to their purchased items without warning, due to their licenses getting revoked. See the attachment for a recent example with Sony. This all also relates closely to the "Stop Destroying Videogames" European Citizens' Initiative. Another issue, though perhaps outside the scope of this act, but I feel is highly related to "Digital Fairness": Unrestricted right to purchase legal digital goods. Recently MasterCard and Visa pressured Steam and itch.io (digital storefronts) to pull collectively thousands of products from sale. Most were adult-oriented, many even pornographic, but some simply featured adult themes, such as abuse and sexuality. Two prominent examples were horror games "Mouthwashing" and "Fear & Hunger", which by their nature of being horror games handled difficult themes. Payment processors shouldn't be allowed to abuse their monopolistic status to restrict the purchase of legal products (especially fiction) on digital storefronts (or elsewhere). Lastly, as a reminder to keep the EU honest, the Mission Letter highlights the following as an issue: "technological developments and increased tracking". The EU's own "digital identity wallet" and "digital euro" both (especially together) fit this issue. And this highly relates to my point of not restricting the right to legal purchases. In worst case scenario, both projects can enable to ever more dangerous anti-consumer practices than any of the specific issue listed in the Mission Letter.
